meta.siteDescription: Portfolio of Dennis Hawran — Gameplay Programmer, freshly minted Master in Game Development.

cta.contactSubtitle: Game jam idea, junior role, or just coffee — I reply quickly.

about.title: About Me
about.subtitle: PLAYER PROFILE
paragraphs: Hey, I'm Dennis. Gameplay Programmer with a Master's in Game Development.

I build systems that feel good to play. Twin-stick combat, tower defense, AR multiplayer, ML-driven Pong paddles — if it's playable, I want to know how it ticks. Six years with Unity, plus Unreal, FUDGE and C++.

One of my favorite projects: a haptic VR room without controllers. Instead of joysticks, real-world objects are mapped one-to-one to their virtual counterparts. Grab a sword in reality and you grab it in VR too. Genuinely feels like magic.

What I otherwise love: game jams. 48 hours, one idea, a team, and at the end something actually runs. No planning committee, no ticket system, just code and coffee. I learned more about gamedev there than in any course.

descriptions.unthroned:
Twin-stick shooter with base-building and tower-defense elements, built over two semesters as our Master's project. You play Dave, a dethroned necromancer reclaiming his brother's empire.

The core system: a day/night cycle where you summon souls during the day to gather resources for you. Sounds like a no-brainer — summon as many as possible. But at night those same souls turn on you, and you fight every single one back. Players set their own difficulty: more souls = more resources = harder nights. Originally summoning was unlimited, but performance forced us to cap it.

Team of three — I was the only dev, plus two 2D artists. My focus was core combat, enemy AI, the summon and upgrade systems — and way too much time in a custom sprite-sorting algorithm. Isometric 2D with lots of movement and angled objects like portals turns render order into an art form. You can still spot the occasional glitch in the trailer, but the bulk of it sits. We also rewrote the controls many times — twin-stick as a genre was settled early, but the feel (aim direction, rotation while shooting, button mapping) only clicked after heavy playtesting.

We also had an external composer on board: a friend from earlier projects who submitted our soundtrack as his own two-semester project at his uni — multiple custom tracks written for us.
descriptions.oasis:
I built Oasis over two semesters. The first was a study project where the game itself came together: an escape room in Unreal with three levels and one puzzle each, still classic controller-based. In the second semester it became my bachelor's thesis, and the real centerpiece arrived — the haptic VR room, the physical installation behind the game.

We'd originally planned the whole thing with a motion-capture suit and gloves. Then the chip shortage hit and the ordered system just never arrived in time. So I pivoted and hacked everything together with HTC Vive Trackers instead — which ended up working surprisingly well.

The core idea: instead of controllers, the player picks up real objects. The university provided a physical room and access to a 3D printer. Puzzle props hung on the walls via magnets, each connected to a tracker that synced its position into VR. To make the virtual world match the real geometry precisely, I scanned the entire room in 3D with my iPad's LIDAR. Those scans let me place every prop in Unreal down to the millimeter — at the start of each session the player just had to tap three calibration points and the whole room snapped into place.

Each level had a weapon — sword, staff, book — and a matching puzzle. With the sword you redirected lasers to arrange floating islands in the right order. With the book you slotted orbs into matching sockets. With the staff you were supposed to guide butterflies, but that last puzzle I didn't finish in time.

The cleverest part of the system were the magnets. In VR you're holding a full sword; physically it's just a grip with a sensor. When you bring the weapon back to its designated spot on the wall, the magnet pulls it into place — feels like the VR world is taking the object out of your hand. The orbs were plastic spheres with sensors inside; their wall-mounted sockets were half-round magnet cups, so the spheres appear to float in midair.

Level design also had to disguise the physical room's limits. My solution: everything happens on floating islands in the sky. Nobody willingly steps into open air, which meant I could place islands only where I wanted the player to go. By cleverly rotating the same floor plan, I could also hide the fact that you stay in one single room the whole time, and that the sword grip and staff grip are physically the same prop.

And finally the little tutorial ghost. A balloon with a fabric cover, mounted to the ceiling, controlled via a motor and a Raspberry Pi. The game told the Pi over a server call when to lower the ghost and when to retract it. During the tutorial the player could actually touch it; once the tutorial was done, it was simply gone — the project where I learned the most about hardware-software integration.
descriptions.aurea:
Aurea stands for Augmented Reality Arena. At its core it's a turn-based monster-battling game for your phone — somewhere between Yu-Gi-Oh and Pokémon, but in AR. You collect monsters and level them up by fighting other players. Each monster has its own abilities, and the interesting twist is position-based synergy: place certain monsters next to certain others, and their abilities amplify each other — setting up formations mattered just as much as the monster collection itself.

The AR angle: the battle plays out on your living-room table, or via AR Foundation takes over your entire garden. You lock in your turn, the monsters play it out, then the opponent's turn — and while that happens you can physically walk around the scene and watch the action from any angle.

Originally, incidentally, the whole thing was supposed to be a machine learning project. It was a requirement from our professor, and we planned the entire first semester around it — including special ML lectures just for our team, so we could train the enemy AI ourselves. Then mid-project: "Hmm, the training timeline is getting tight, how about we make it multiplayer instead?" It was a brutal pivot, but it actually worked in the end.

We built the whole thing with six people — three programmers, three artists — across two semesters. I was one of the programmers and mostly worked on AR plane detection, multiplayer synchronization between players, and the overall gameplay feel. The main thing I learned: AR is exhausting. Multiplayer is a lot more exhausting. Doing both at once in a student project without completely drowning is non-trivial. Despite that — or maybe because of it — Aurea ended up winning a media prize at our university.
descriptions.littleCritters:
Little Critters is our submission for the Cozy Fall Jam 2024, and the game aims to be exactly what the jam name promises: pure relaxation. Warm autumn ambience, music that invites you to stay a while, and no pressure otherwise.

You wander through the woods collecting trash, chestnuts, and other found bits, and assemble them into your own little creatures — critters. There are three modes: a classic one with limited resources, a somewhat freer one, and a creative mode where you can do whatever you like.

The project came together with a larger team than usual — seven of us: two 2D artists, three 3D artists, one audio designer, and me on programming. Built in Unity, playable in the browser or as a download for Windows and Linux.

And if you look closely, you'll find a rare special mushroom here, and picking one up makes the whole game go, briefly, surprisingly… colorful.
descriptions.itsNotRocketScience:
It's Not Rocket Science is our Easter-break jam from 2026 — just without an official jam. We wanted to jam over the holidays and picked our own theme. After talking through a few ideas, this was the one we felt like building.

You fly a small spaceship through the galaxy looking for your home planet. Not as easy as it sounds: your fuel's running out, comets are crossing your path, and if you drift too close to planets or stars their gravity pulls you into a crash. Along the way you'll find shops with upgrades — a compass that marks nearby planets, a trajectory visualizer that shows your flight curve before you commit, a shield that lets you survive a collision. There are also mechanics that upgrade your modules: shield, mining, solar, compass, trajectory. The resources for all that come from orbiting planets with your mining module active.

Technically this was mostly a performance story. The level is huge and every planet, star, asteroid, and comet exists in the scene at once — several thousand objects. To keep it running I built a grid system where objects register themselves automatically and get toggled on or off depending on the player's position. The trajectory visualizer was the second performance battle: every frame it simulates several hundred points into the future to show you your flight curve. A lot of optimization work went into that too.

Team of four, me on programming. Built in Unity, playable in the browser or as a Windows download.

And hidden in space too: look closely and you'll find a rare special mushroom, and picking one up makes the whole game go, briefly, surprisingly… colorful.
descriptions.kibosPath:
Kibō's Path is our submission for the Cozy Winter Jam 2026 — a slider game where you guide a mother polar bear and her cub down a snow slope, dodging obstacles and working your way to safety.

Between the levels, cut scenes tell the story behind it: a collapsing ice landscape, mother and cub on the run, themes around climate change and polar bear conservation for International Polar Bear Day.

The biggest technical challenge was the controls: levels feature normal slopes, but also half-pipes where you jump and closed pipes where you can even slide along the ceiling. All three variants needed to feel clean and intuitive — considerably trickier than it looks in the final build.

My solution was a spline-driven system. The pipes get placed automatically along the spline and the meshes bend accordingly. At defined positions I can snap prefabs onto the spline — obstacles, transitions, level props. And because the spline itself knew where each half-pipe and closed-pipe section was, it doubled as a guidance layer: the system could detect on the fly whether the player should be jumping, plain sliding, or sticking to the ceiling, and the controls adapted accordingly.

Team of four: the jam crew, me on programming. The game was built in Unity and runs in the browser or as a download for Windows and Linux.
descriptions.sockItUp:
Sock It Up! is our submission for the Novice Summer Jam Series 2025: Short Jam 3. The theme was "One Button Input", and our take on it: the player has a single master button plus a series of commands spread around the screen. Drag the master button onto one of those commands and it takes that command's role — press it, and exactly that action fires.

That's how you control your sock puppet: walk, jump, or rotate the world. Certain paths only open up when the level is in the right orientation, so the puzzle is part platformer, part constant rethinking of what you need next and in what order.

The look is deliberately playful, like a colorful children's toy world. Team of four: the jam crew, assembled a little smaller this time. I was programmer again. The game was built in Unity and runs either in the browser or as a Windows download.
descriptions.professorPapillon:
Professor Papillon is our submission for the Cozy Spring Jam 2025, and once again it's deliberately designed as pure relaxation. A small garden, springtime ambience, music that floats along in the background — not much more is needed.

You're given the garden and plant different kinds of flowers to attract butterflies. The twist: certain plants don't get along with their neighbors and wilt if you set them too close together. So there's a bit of strategic placement involved to get the garden looking right.

We built it as a team of eight — the recurring jam crew of 2D and 3D artists, plus audio design. This time I was on programming. The game runs in the browser or as a Windows download.

And just like in a few of my other jam games, there's a well-hidden extra: look closely and you'll find a rare special mushroom, and if you pick one up the whole game becomes, briefly, surprisingly… colorful.
descriptions.cozyWinter:
Cozy Winter Market came together in three days for the Cozy Winter Jam — part of the Cozy XXX jam series I've entered a handful of over the years — with a music designer on board. The theme was "Connections". My take: a Christmas market as a small network of stalls, decorations and visitors, all feeding into each other.

The principle is as relaxed as it gets. You build a Christmas market and attract visitors. More stalls bring more visitors, more decoration makes them happier, and happy visitors spend more money. Every now and then random gifts spawn that give you a bit of extra cash. No fail state, no timer — just a loop that feels good.

The pixel art came from a ready-made asset pack — the only way to fit gameplay scope into three days. I handled code, game design and balancing; the music designer owned the entire sonic side, which ended up being the key ingredient for the cozy vibe.
descriptions.pumpkinHarvest:
Pumpkin Harvest is a three-day jam project for the Cozy Fall Jam. Vibe-wise: Stardew Valley with a bit of extra footwork.

You plant, water, and harvest pumpkins — but you can only carry one at a time. So: harvest, run across town to a customer, drop off, head back. Each customer tells you how many pumpkins they want, and you shuttle back and forth until the order is full. Farm long enough and you can afford a car that fits three pumpkins at once. (Or four, if you exploit the bug that lets you climb in while already holding one. Never fixed it — kind of charming.)

Built entirely solo, using 3D assets from a Kenney pack and some sound libraries. No epic scope, but a self-contained gameplay loop — harvest, run, deliver, upgrade — and exactly the right size for three days.
descriptions.witchWay:
Witch Way? came out of a two-day jam I organized for my semester at uni. We earned course credit for building a game in jam format and presenting it afterwards — and enough people showed up for two teams.

The theme: you don't control the player, you control the world. Our witch flies on her own through a level of hexagon tiles, and you can't tell her anything directly — instead you hold a hand of cards that you place on the hexes. Some turn her left or right, others drop bridges over gaps, spawn mushrooms for her to bounce off, or give her a healing potion that makes her briefly invincible. The cards you draw are random, so each level becomes a spontaneous puzzle: play what you've got now, or wait for a better hand?

And then there are the rare mushrooms that occasionally appear. Pick one up and the whole game becomes, briefly, surprisingly… colorful.
descriptions.gravityPong:
Gravity Pong was my foray into reinforcement learning. I'd tried it before on more complex games and nothing really converged, so this time I went deliberately small: a simple game with a single twist.

The twist: Pong, but the gravity shifts every so often. Your paddle gets pulled up or down, even when you're trying to move it the other way. Sounds like a small change, but it makes the timing much messier — and forces the agent to react not only to the ball but to the drift of its own paddle.

Training went surprisingly well — I ended up with several difficulty levels. Funnily enough, that was the trickiest part: how do you deliberately make an agent worse? My approach was to train one agent that played cleanly, then throttle its action space for the easier tiers — for example, only letting it execute every second move it wanted to make. The specifics are a bit blurry now, but the idea was: don't train-badly on purpose, artificially limit the good agent.

Hobby project, fully solo, a couple of weeks in total. Not groundbreaking, but the first time I saw a model I'd trained myself actually learn something — and that taught me more about RL than every tutorial had before.
descriptions.bomberman:
Bomberman was my final project for the "Development of Interactive Applications" course at Furtwangen University. The engine was mandated: FUDGE, HFU's in-house JavaScript/TypeScript engine. Back then it was still quite early-stage — no editor UI, almost no tutorials, very little community material. A lot of reading engine docs and figuring things out by trial.

On the content side I wanted to recreate a classic, and Bomberman fit perfectly: a compact grid, clear rules, bomb-driven chain reactions — a scope I could actually finish. One or two weeks, solo.

The project was less spectacular than it was educational. Without engine conveniences you realize quickly how many systems a game actually needs — game loop, input handling, collisions, rendering — and how much of that Unity and Unreal usually just hand to you. Runs directly in the browser.
descriptions.coffeeExe:
Top-secret long-running project: reliably convert coffee into runnable code. Shipping since 1996, crashes without espresso, performance scales proportionally to bean strength.

Only known balance issue: excessive sugar introduces unexpected features. Pull requests (in bean form) welcome.
